content: I'm designing a Kubernetes operator architecture that brings NVIDIA DPU support into the vendor-neutral OPI DPU operator, while maximizing reuse of NVIDIA's own DPF (DOCA Platform Framework) operator. I need this to hold up as an elite, production-grade design: strict multi-vendor separation, explicit failure-domain handling, and lightweight resource usage on a constrained DPU control plane. Propose the core integration pattern first, before we get into failure handling.

Use an adapter pattern with a dynamic plugin registry, not a sub-operator or sidecar. Define a single generic interface, e.g. OpiProviderPlugin, with methods Name(), Supports(nodeLabels), Translate(profile) and Status(ctx, client, profile). The OPI core reconciler only ever talks to this interface -- it never imports NVIDIA, Intel, or Marvell types directly (Open-Closed Principle). Each vendor driver self-registers into a factory registry at operator boot, keyed off Node Feature Discovery labels -- for NVIDIA silicon that's something like feature.node.kubernetes.io/pci-0300_10de.present. The reconciler asks the registry 'who supports this node?' and delegates, rather than branching on vendor identity anywhere in the core loop. Running the adapter as a compiled-in Go package inside the single OPI binary (no sidecar, no extra pod) keeps the DPU control plane's memory footprint minimal, since DPUs run hyper-constrained control planes.

For the CRD design: should one OPI-level custom resource map to a single DPF CRD, or could it expand into more than one? NVIDIA DPF actually exposes both a node-level DPUSet CRD (firmware/flavor provisioning) and a DPUService CRD (workload/service deployment). Which should the adapter target?

Target both DPUSet and DPUService from a single top-level OPI CRD. Real DPU lifecycle has two distinct phases -- provisioning the DPU itself (firmware, OS image, flavor) and deploying a workload/service onto it once provisioned -- and collapsing that into one downstream object would force artificial coupling between the two phases. Keep the OPI CRD itself vendor-neutral: it should express intent only ('enable load balancing offload', 'attach flavor X'), and the adapter's Translate() function is responsible for expanding that intent into the concrete DPUSet and DPUService objects. The OPI CRD must never carry NVIDIA-specific fields -- if DPF needs an extra parameter, the adapter computes it internally, not the schema.
What should we call the top-level OPI CRD Kind?

OpiDpuProfile reads clearly as vendor-neutral intent (a 'profile' of desired DPU capabilities), doesn't imply a specific vendor's terminology, and won't collide with DPF's own Kind names (DPUSet, DPUService), which keeps the two schemas visually distinct in manifests and logs.
Now the failure domains. Walk through what happens if the cluster is under heavy load, if a write fails mid-operation, and if the NVIDIA DPF operator crashes entirely.

Heavy load: wrap the controller's workqueue in a token-bucket rate limiter, and cap MaxConcurrentReconciles at a small number (2 is a reasonable default) so a fleet-wide node-label change doesn't spike CPU usage on constrained DPU control planes. Mid-write failure: make Translate() a pure, deterministic function of OpiDpuProfile -- rerunning it after a partial failure reproduces the same desired object set, so the next reconcile naturally resumes without duplicating resources. Also never use client.Update (full overwrite); always use client.Patch with server-side apply, so a concurrent write from DPF's own controller doesn't get clobbered -- a three-way merge only touches the fields OPI actually owns. DPF crashing entirely: have the adapter check, each reconcile, whether the DPUSet/DPUService CRD definitions still exist via the discovery client. If they vanish, set OpiDpuProfile.status to Degraded with reason DependencyMissing: NVIDIA DPF operator unresponsive, and requeue with exponential backoff -- 5s, 10s, 30s, 60s -- rather than hot-looping, so DPF has room to self-heal.
What concurrency number should MaxConcurrentReconciles actually use, and why not just maximize throughput?

2. DPU-hosted control planes have very little spare CPU/memory budget -- unlike a normal cluster control plane, the OPI operator is often running on or adjacent to the same constrained hardware it's managing. A higher worker count optimizes for reconcile throughput at the cost of starving the node during simultaneous mass updates (e.g. a fleet-wide firmware rollout), which is the higher-risk failure mode here. 2 is a deliberate floor above 1 (to avoid head-of-line blocking) without chasing throughput the hardware can't safely absorb.

Not every feature will have parity across vendors. Design the 'degraded feature matrix' behavior using a concrete example: something Intel's offload stack supports today that NVIDIA DPF doesn't yet expose.

Use eBPF-based programmable load balancing as the example -- Intel's stack supports it, current NVIDIA DPF does not expose it. When a profile requests it, the adapter should not hard-fail the reconcile. Instead: detect the gap against DPF's capability matrix, drop the eBPF LB request, and fall back to DPF's standard HBN (Host-Based Networking) pipeline filter rules to deliver basic load balancing at reduced fidelity. Emit a Kubernetes Warning Event -- FeatureDegraded: eBPF load balancing unsupported on NVIDIA DPF, falling back to HBN filters -- and record the gap in OpiDpuProfile.status.conditions. This keeps the DPU functional rather than fully blocked, while making the silent downgrade visible to operators instead of pretending full parity exists.
How should deletion be handled so we don't leave orphaned DPU-side configuration behind on the hardware?

Set an OwnerReference (with BlockOwnerDeletion: true) from every generated DPUSet and DPUService object back to its parent OpiDpuProfile at creation time. When the OpiDpuProfile is deleted, Kubernetes' own garbage collector cascades the deletion to both owned objects atomically -- DPF then tears down the firmware provisioning and the deployed service in response to those deletion events. This means the adapter needs no bespoke cleanup reconcile logic at all; it's delegated entirely to native K8s garbage collection.
The assignment allows targeting NVIDIA or AMD. Since we chose NVIDIA as the primary track, should AMD be addressed at all, and if so how, without expanding scope?

Address it as an extensibility proof, not a second implementation. Because the core reconciler only depends on the OpiProviderPlugin interface and the NFD-label-keyed registry, adding AMD later is a registration exercise, not a core change: when a node carries AMD-specific NFD labels, the factory would load an AmdPensandoAdapter that translates the same neutral OpiDpuProfile into AMD Distributed Services Architecture (DSA) configuration objects instead of DOCA-based manifests. Zero changes needed to the reconciler, the registry, or the CRD schema. This is worth one section in the design doc as validation of the Strict Interface Separation rule -- it shouldn't dilute the NVIDIA-focused implementation that's the actual deliverable.
Give me the full set of sequence diagrams needed to document this end-to-end: happy path, the failure/backoff behavior, cascade deletion, and the degraded-feature fallback.

Four Mermaid sequenceDiagrams covers the full operational lifecycle without redundancy: (1) Happy-path reconcile -- OpiDpuProfile applied, registry resolves NvidiaDpfAdapter, Translate() produces DPUSet+DPUService, both patched via server-side apply, DPF provisions and reports Ready, status propagates back up. (2) Failure/backoff -- a patch succeeds but the DPF CRDs are later found missing on discovery check, status goes Degraded, backoff climbs 5s->10s->30s->60s, and recovery resumes from live state once DPF's CRDs reappear. (3) Cascade deletion -- OpiDpuProfile delete triggers K8s GC to remove both owned objects via OwnerReferences, DPF deprovisions in response, no orphaned hardware config remains. (4) Degraded-feature-drop -- Translate() detects the eBPF LB gap against DPF's capability matrix, falls back to HBN filters, patches the fallback object, and emits both the Warning Event and the status condition. Each diagram should stay to a single scenario rather than trying to combine happy-path and failure handling into one, so they're each independently legible.

Finally, give me a Go module skeleton for this -- controller-runtime based, package main, using only standard upstream dependencies (no proprietary/vendor-private imports), representing the DPF objects generically enough that it doesn't require NVIDIA's private DPF Go types to compile.

Use sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime and sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime/pkg/client for the manager/reconciler/patch surface, and k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/apis/meta/v1 plus k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime for the object metadata and scheme plumbing. Represent DPUSet and DPUService as unstructured.Unstructured objects built from GVK + a map[string]interface{} spec, rather than importing DPF's own generated Go types -- that's what lets the file compile cleanly with go mod tidy without pulling in any private vendor schema. Structure it as: the OpiProviderPlugin interface, an OpiDpuProfile struct with a minimal Spec/Status (as a local type, not a full CRD codegen), a ProviderRegistry with Register/Lookup, a NvidiaDpfAdapter implementing Translate() to build the two unstructured objects and Status() to check discovery for CRD presence, a setOwnerReference helper, a GenerationChangedPredicate wiring example, and an OpiDpuProfileReconciler with MaxConcurrentReconciles: 2 and an exponential requeue helper for the 5/10/30/60s backoff. package main with a minimal main() wiring the manager is fine since this is a skeleton, not the final cmd/ layout.
Verify our assumed CRD names, API groups, and node labels directly against the upstream opiproject/dpu-operator repository to ensure we are not submitting fictional constraints.

Cross-checking confirms that openshift/dpu-operator and opiproject/dpu-operator are the same codebase. The real core CRD is DpuOperatorConfig (config.openshift.io/v1) and the node gate is a plain dpu=true label, not an NFD namespace. Real-world vendor mapping is executed via separate runtime VSP daemons. We will preserve our OpiDpuProfile interface registry as a lightweight architectural alternative, update our code constants to target the real dpu=true gate, and explicitly flag the PCI class codes as unverified environment variables to maintain absolute engineering integrity.
